completeness.rationale: Captured the key items: size-dependent behavior (small PDFs fine, large ones corrupt), onset ~1 week ago, multiple customers affected, immediate corruption on re-download, and web interface as the upload method. However, the agent never asked whether other large file types (images, spreadsheets) were also affected — a significant gap given that the ground truth confirms they are. The 1MB threshold wasn't established, though the reporter couldn't supply exact file sizes so this is partly a reporter limitation.

accuracy.rationale: The triage follows the first acceptable diagnostic path clearly: it identified size-dependent behavior and pointed to the upload pipeline (specifically mentioning 'a misconfigured reverse proxy, CDN, or middleware' and 'binary-vs-text encoding regression') as probable causes. The actual cause is a proxy stripping Content-Type causing text/plain encoding on binary data — the agent's hypotheses explicitly include both the proxy and encoding regression, which together describe the actual mechanism. Not exact, but meaningfully close and on the right track.

thoroughness.rationale: The agent asked sensible questions and correctly identified the size clue as a strong lead to close on. However, an obvious follow-up was left on the table: asking whether other large file types (images, spreadsheets) also corrupt would have helped isolate whether this is PDF-specific or a general large-file issue. The agent also never directly asked whether IT had noticed any recent deployments or configuration changes, instead inferring from the reporter's 'something changed on your end' comment.

economy.rationale: Three questions across four reporter turns. Each question served a distinct diagnostic purpose: onset/type (Q1), corruption nature and timing (Q2), size range and upload method (Q3). Q2 had multiple sub-questions but they were tightly related and efficient to group. No obvious redundancy or wasted turns.

question_quality.rationale: The second question — asking whether corruption happens immediately on re-download vs. later — was genuinely insightful: it confirmed server-side corruption and ruled out client-side issues. The file-size-comparison question (asking if the downloaded file seemed smaller) demonstrated real diagnostic thinking about truncation. The questions weren't purely generic; they reflected a specific mental model about upload pipeline failures.

actionability.rationale: The triage summary is exceptionally actionable: it includes a concrete reproduction procedure with specific file size targets, a binary-diff recommendation to characterize the corruption type, explicit pointers to check the reverse proxy and content-type handling in the deploy history, and a proposed automated integration test. A developer could begin coding a fix or running diagnostics immediately.

notable_strengths: Correctly identified size-dependent behavior as the pivotal diagnostic clue and pursued it decisively
Root cause hypotheses explicitly included reverse proxy misconfiguration and binary-vs-text encoding regression — which together describe the actual mechanism
The question about immediate vs. delayed corruption was diagnostically sharp: it confirmed server-side causation before asking any infrastructure questions
Triage summary is highly actionable with concrete reproduction steps, binary-diff recommendation, and specific areas of the stack to investigate
notable_weaknesses: Never asked whether other large file types (images, spreadsheets) were also affected — this would have been a strong signal that corruption is not PDF-specific but size/path-specific
Did not directly ask the reporter to confirm or check for any recent deployments or system changes (the agent inferred this from the reporter's comment rather than probing it)
The 1MB threshold was not established, though this is partly a reporter limitation (they couldn't provide file sizes)
most_insightful_question: When you download one of these corrupted PDFs back from TaskFlow, is it already broken at that point — or does the corruption seem to happen later? (This confirmed immediate/server-side corruption and ruled out client-side or deferred processing issues.)
missed_opportunities: Ask whether other large file types — images, Word documents, spreadsheets — also come through broken, to test whether the issue is PDF-specific or affects all large files
Ask the reporter's IT contact (or prompt them to check with IT) whether any deployment, configuration change, or infrastructure update was made approximately one week ago
Ask the reporter to try uploading the same large PDF via a different method (e.g., mobile app or API if available) to help isolate whether the bug is in the web upload path specifically
